headline: Brazil's Xokleng People Restore Sacred Pine Trees and Their Culture
meta_description: The Zág Institute is replanting nearly 100,000 Brazilian pine seedlings across indigenous territory in Santa Catarina, restoring both a critically endangered s…
summary_short: The Xokleng people are reviving their sacred Brazilian pine forests, planting 100,000 seedlings across 1,000 hectares to strengthen their endangered culture and heal deg…
body:
For the Xokleng people of Santa Catarina state, the Brazilian pine—called zág in their language—is far more than timber. It appears in their creation myths, gives names to their people, and represents the heart of who they are. When Carl Gakran, executive director of the Zág Institute, learned the species had become critically endangered, occupying less than 3% of its original range, he felt compelled to act. What began in 2016 as a personal effort to plant ten seedlings on his home plot has grown into a community-wide restoration project that has already produced more than 140,000 seedlings, with roughly 100,000 planted across 1,000 hectares of the Ibirama-La Klãnõ Indigenous Territory. "Just as our pine is endangered, my people are also endangered, and consequently our language and culture are endangered," Carl explains. The reforestation work has restored water sources and degraded land while keeping the community at its center—families adopt seedlings as if they were pets, children receive their own trees to nurture, and women make up half the active participants, earning recognition as "the women who plant zág."

The institute's approach honors Indigenous knowledge at every step. Harvesters gather seeds following traditional rituals, respectfully collecting only 60% of cones to ensure animals and the forest itself remain nourished. Schools and village associations participate in collective planting days during school holidays, with 30 to 40 regular volunteers growing to 60 during harvest season. This isn't just environmental restoration—it's cultural survival through action.

Some planted areas sit on territories contested in Brazil's Supreme Federal Court, but the Xokleng persist. As Isabel, Carl's wife and the institute's environmental director, notes: "As a way of demarcating our territory, we also plant trees in those areas." In restoring zág, they are restoring themselves.
quick_facts: Nearly 100,000 Brazilian pine seedlings planted across 1,000 hectares in Santa Catarina
Zág Institute led by Carl Gakran, Xokleng executive director, since 2016
Brazilian pine covers less than 3% of its original range due to deforestation
About 2,000 Xokleng people live in 10 villages on the restored territory
Women comprise half of the institute's active participants in planting efforts
